deals_cleared.merger_description: Allied Retail Finance Pty Ltd (**Allied Retail Finance**), a wholly owned subsidiary of Allied Credit Pty Ltd (**Allied Credit**), proposes to acquire the remaining portfolio of automotive loans and leases originated by Macquarie Leasing Pty Limited and other associated assets (including all legal and equitable interests in the data and records relating to the loan assets) held by Macquarie Leasing Pty Ltd, Macquarie Bank Limited and the trustee of the SMART B Trust (together, **Macquarie**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Allied Retail Finance Pty Ltd (Allied Retail Finance), a wholly owned
subsidiary of Allied Credit Pty Ltd (Allied Credit), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of
automotive loans and leases originated by Macquarie Leasing Pty
Limited and other associated assets (including all legal and
equitable interests in the data and records relating to the loan
assets) held by Macquarie Leasing Pty Limited, Macquarie Bank
Limited and the trustee of the SMART B Trust (together, Macquarie),
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Allied Credit was established in 2010 and provides motorcycle,
marine, recreation and automotive finance solutions to vehicle and
equipment businesses, original equipment manufacturers and
distributor dealer networks and their customers in Australia. Allied
Credit's product range includes retail consumer, commercial, and
wholesale floorplan finance, as well as novated leases.
Macquarie is a global financial services group operating in asset
management, retail and business banking, wealth management, as
well as advisory, and risk and capital solutions across debt, equity,
financial markets and commodities.
Macquarie has ceased originating retail automotive financing loans.
Macquarie's staged exit from the automotive finance industry began
in 2021 (Macquarie will continue to service a small portfolio of
ongoing car loans but ceased new car loan originations in April
2024). Prior to its exit, Macquarie provided car loans, asset finance,
and novated leasing to personal and business clients.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between Allied Credit and
Macquarie in the supply of retail automative finance in
Australia
b. there are alternative suppliers of retail automative finance in
Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified. The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent
with the object of the Act and the interests of consumers in
promoting competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: AVC Operations Pty Ltd (**AVC**) proposes to acquire substantially all of the assets of the licensed hotel trading as the White Cockatoo Hotel (including the exclusive right to carry on the business in succession of the vendor and the benefit of the lease) from Petersham Pub Pty Ltd (**Vendor**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Australian Venue Co. Holdings Limited (Australian Venue Co), via
AVC Operations Pty Limited (AVC), applied for a notification waiver
in respect of its proposed acquisition of substantially all of the
assets of the licensed hotel trading as the White Cockatoo Hotel
(WCH) (including the exclusive right to carry on the business in
succession of the vendor and the benefit of the lease) from
Petersham Pub Pty Ltd, as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, AVC, is a wholly owned subsidiary of Australian Venue
Co. Australian Venue Co operates over 200 licensed venues
throughout Australia and New Zealand, primarily pubs, as well as
bars, clubs and licensed restaurants. Their venues are individually
branded and mostly located in inner-city and metro areas, offering
food, alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages, function rooms, live
entertainment and gaming facilities.
The target, WCH, is a licensed hotel with no gaming facilities, located
at 30 Terminus Street, Petersham NSW 2049. WCH facilities include
a public bar, bistro and outdoor courtyard.
Australian Venue Co, through its venues (including Public House
Petersham), and WCH both supply retail hospitality services in the
local area surrounding WCH.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver and had regard
to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
• while there is horizontal overlap between Australian Venue
Co and WCH, the combined share of the parties in the supply
of retail hospitality services is low
• there are alternative suppliers of retail hospitality venues in
the local area surrounding WCH.

The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
CRH Plc (CRH), via CRH Americas Products, Inc. (CRH Americas)
and Oldcastle Infrastructure, Inc. (Oldcastle), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of
the partnership interests and voting rights in Axius Water Parent LP
(Axius Water), as described in the transaction documents provided
as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The ultimate acquirer, CRH, is a global supplier of building materials
and infrastructure products headquartered in Ireland and listed on
the New York Stock Exchange. CRH Americas and Oldcastle would
have sole control of Axius Water.
CRH Americas is an intermediate holding company which does not
directly manufacture or supply goods or services. Oldcastle is a US-
based supplier of engineered concrete products related to water,
communications, energy and transportation infrastructure with a
focus on sustainability. It has no presence in Australia.
CTC Precast Pty Ltd (Civilmart), which is indirectly owned by CRH, is
a supplier of precast concrete infrastructure products in Australia,
including stormwater and drainage components, stormwater quality
improvement devices, sewer chambers and pump stations, and
domestic aerated wastewater treatment systems.
Hydro International is a CRH subsidiary that supplies water filtration
products, including sludge screens and stormwater separators and
filters, into Australia
The target, Axius Water, is a US based supplier of nutrient removal
solutions and process-intensive treatment for municipal and
industrial waste-water and water treatment processes via brands
including Aero-Mod, atac, EDI, Eosi, MITAwt, Napier-Reid, Nexom and
triplepoint. Axius Water has minimal export sales into Australia and
no operations, assets, employees or subsidiaries in Australia.
CRH and its controlled portfolio companies, and Axius Water, both
supply wastewater treatment solutions in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. Axius Water has a small presence in Australia
b. while there may be a small horizontal or complementary
relationship between the products and services supplied by
CRH (including its controlled portfolio companies) and Axius
Water, there are alternative suppliers of these products and
services in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

DGH Design and Fabrication Services Pty Ltd (DGH), a wholly owned

The acquirer, DGH, is a fabrication and metal engineering company
providing metal engineering, drafting, fabrication, fitting, machining,
blast, and painting services, with branches in Mackay, Emerald, and
Townsville. It does not have a branch in the Gladstone region but
supplies Cement Australia in Gladstone from its Mackay branch.
The target, GMG, is a fabrication and metal engineering company
providing steel fabrication and coating services for all types of metal
across the industrial manufacturing industry, with workshops in
Maryborough and Gladstone. The Acquisition relates only to GMG’s
workshop in Gladstone.
The parties both supply metal fabrication and engineering services,
including coating services, in the Gladstone region.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material

a. there is small horizontal overlap between the parties in the
supply of metal fabrication and engineering services in the
Gladstone region, and their combined share of supply is
small
b. while there is a vertical relationship between the parties and
DGH’s parent company, Cement Australia, there appears to
be low risk of vertical foreclosure arising from the
Acquisition due to the presence of alternative suppliers and
customers of metal fabrication and engineering services,
including in Gladstone.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely

deals_cleared.merger_description: eBay Inc. (**eBay**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Depop Limited (**Depop**) from Etsy, Inc (**Etsy**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: eBay - Depop - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
eBay Inc. (eBay)’s proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital
in Depop Limited (Depop) (together, the Parties) from Etsy, Inc.
(Etsy) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, eBay, is a multinational e‑commerce company
headquartered in San Jose, California. eBay operates a general
online retail marketplace, facilitating both business‑to‑consumer
(B2C) and consumer‑to‑consumer (C2C) transactions across a wide
range of new and used product categories, including fashion,
electronics, automotive parts and collectibles.
The target, Depop, is a multinational e‑commerce company
headquartered in London and a wholly owned subsidiary of Etsy.
Depop operates a mobile‑first online retail marketplace, focused on
pre‑owned fashion, with an emphasis on social discovery and
peer‑to‑peer transactions. Depop is predominantly active in the
United States, Australia and the United Kingdom.
The reasons for the ACCC’s determination are set out in section 2.

determination_statement_of_reasons.text: When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.
For the reasons given below, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect.
Overlap between the parties

eBay and Depop both provide online marketplaces that enable sellers to create listings for pre-owned fashion items (for example, clothing, shoes, accessories, handbags and watches) and buyers to search for and purchase those items.

eBay operates a general online retail marketplace that includes pre-owned fashion as a category, as well as a broad range of other product categories, such as electronics, automotive parts and collectibles. It offers multiple selling formats, including fixed price, live selling, and auctions. eBay also provides integrated payments and delivery options, buyer protection policies and seller performance standards. eBay has operated in Australia since 1999.

Depop is a mobile-first online retail marketplace that focuses on pre-owned fashion. It has a range of social discovery features, such as algorithmically curated feeds and the ability for buyers to follow other users and ‘like’ items.

Depop is primarily oriented towards C2C transactions, although there is a cohort of business sellers that use Depop regularly. Sales typically take place through a series of in-app ‘offers’, and Depop provides integrated payments, with sellers in Australia arranging shipping themselves. Depop has operated in Australia since 2020.
Industry background

Online marketplaces that include categories for pre-owned fashion typically involve a digital platform that provides a website or application (‘app’), which allows sellers to list items for sale. Sellers upload information regarding the product for sale including size (or measurements), condition (e.g., Like-New, Excellent, Good), brand, photographs, and a price. Potential purchasers can generally search by keyword or browse by category, with different search filters available depending on product type (e.g., Size, Condition, etc.).

Online marketplaces may facilitate sales through taking payment, or buyers and sellers may arrange to meet in person and process the sale outside the platform. Items listed for sale on the digital platform may come primarily from business sellers or consumer sellers, or from a mix of both. Similarly, while there are two distinct user groups: buyers and sellers, many individuals who buy items will also sell items, and they may do so using the same platform.

Online marketplaces can be general (offering a broad range of product categories) or specialised in one category (e.g. fashion, cars, watches). eBay, Facebook Marketplace and Gumtree are examples of general online marketplaces, while Depop, Vestiaire Collective, Chrono24, StockX and GOAT are examples of platforms which focus on a particular product category (i.e., fashion).

Online marketplaces are also multi-sided, which means they create value from attracting both sellers (inventory) and buyers (audience). As multi-sided platforms, they exhibit network effects—more listings draw more buyers, which in turn attracts more sellers. As inventory and audience numbers increase on a platform, this improves the platform’s ability to attract more users to its site and increase the number of items available for sale.
At the same time, user preferences are heterogeneous, and platforms may differentiate successfully by focusing on distinct categories or customer needs.

Users of online marketplaces may multi-home, and this may occur on both the buyer side and seller side (for example, a buyer browsing multiple platforms for the best price or buying different types of clothes from different platforms, or a seller maintaining listings across more than one platform at a time). However, multi-homing is more frictionless for buyers than for sellers, because sellers typically must create listings, manage queries, update item availability and coordinate shipping and returns on each platform they use.

Competition between online marketplaces occurs across multiple dimensions, including in relation to fees, ease of listing, search and discovery functionality, trust and safety settings, and shipping and payment options.
Competition assessment

The ACCC has considered the effects of the Acquisition by comparing the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition proceeds against the likely future state of competition if the Acquisition does not proceed.
Relevant areas of competition

The ACCC has considered whether the Acquisition is likely to substantially lessen competition in the supply of online marketplace services for C2C pre-owned fashion to sellers and/or buyers in Australia.

The ACCC has had regard to information and documents provided by the Parties (including demographic data on eBay and Depop sellers) as part of its assessment. However, the ACCC did not find it necessary to reach a concluded view on whether online marketplace services for C2C pre-owned fashion to sellers and/or buyers constitutes a distinct market or a segment of a broader market.
Horizontal unilateral effects in the supply of online marketplace services for C2C pre-owned fashion in Australia

The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in relation to the supply of online marketplace services for C2C pre-owned fashion in Australia. The ACCC finds that, while the Acquisition would remove competition between eBay and Depop, post-acquisition, eBay would continue to face competition from alternative suppliers
The ACCC’s view, based on the information and documents provided by the Parties and market participants, is that:

items: There are several online marketplaces which feature C2C pre-owned fashion, but eBay and Depop are currently the largest suppliers that offer buyers and sellers integrated services for listing, messaging and payments.

Since entering Australia in 2020, Depop has rapidly increased its market share through a differentiated business model compared to other online marketplaces, with a stronger focus on discovery and social media.
eBay has more recently focused on growing its C2C business, including through eBay’s acquisition of Tise in 2025, and subsequent expansion of Tise into Australia in 2026.

eBay and Depop monitor and respond to each other in Australia. However, there is limited overlap between eBay and Depop pre-owned fashion sellers: during the last 3 years, only a small proportion of sellers on eBay sold a pre-owned fashion item on Depop in the same year, and vice versa.

eBay and Depop are also somewhat differentiated in terms of their format and users—eBay is more search-driven with an older demographic, whereas Depop is more discovery-driven with a younger demographic.

While network effects may create barriers to entry and expansion, online marketplaces for pre-owned fashion are a growing and dynamic segment, with competition driven in part by each platform’s ability to attract and retain new users.
The likely, timely and sufficient entry of a new platform will ensure eBay continues to face competition from alternative suppliers.

AustralianSuper Pty Ltd as trustee for AustralianSuper

proposed acquisition of additional interests in the IFM Global
Infrastructure (Australia) Trust (IFM GIAT), a feeder fund to the IFM
Global Infrastructure Fund (GIF Master Fund), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, AustralianSuper, is an Australian industry
superannuation and pension fund. It offers its members services
relating to superannuation, investment, retirement, and insurance. It
has over 3.6 million members and manages over $410 billion in
funds (as at 31 December 2025).
The target, IFM GIAT, is a fund established for the purpose of
investing in the GIF Master Fund. The GIF Master Fund is a global
infrastructure fund that invests in long-duration, illiquid core
infrastructure assets across transport, utilities, energy transmission
and digital infrastructure.
Both AustralianSuper and GIF Master Fund have interests in Sydney
Airport. AustralianSuper also has an interest in Perth Airport.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the increment to AustralianSuper’s existing interest in IFM
GIAT, and the GIF Master Fund, is small
b. while there is common ownership between AustralianSuper
and GIF Master Fund’s investments in Sydney Airport, the
increment to AustralianSuper’s existing interest is very small
c. while there is cross-ownership between AustralianSuper’s
interests in Sydney Airport and Perth Airport, there is limited
competition between airports.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Unite Holdco LLC (Unite Holdco), an entity owned and controlled by
funds managed by 26North Partners LP (the 26North Funds), applied
for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition,
indirectly via a subsidiary, of 100% of the issued and outstanding
shares of Intermedia Cloud Communications, Inc. (Intermedia), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, the 26North Funds, was established in 2024 as
26North Partners LP’s flagship fund. 26North Partners LP is an
integrated, multi-asset-class investment platform that provides
investment advice and opportunities to its clients across private
equity, credit, insurance and reinsurance strategies.
The 26North Funds has interests in various businesses which supply
goods and services in Australia, including a majority, controlling
interest in AVI-SPL Global LLC (AVI-SPL). AVI-SPL supplies digital
workplace services including multi-office collaboration and audio
video technologies and managed audio video services.
The target, Intermedia, operates a global business supplying
cloud-based communications and collaboration software services to
business customers. These services primarily relate to cloud
telephony voice services, as well as video conferencing, messaging,
email, file sharing, archiving, security and related cloud-based tools.
In Australia, Intermedia’s products and services are supplied via
resellers.
Intermedia and AVI-SPL both supply products and services related to
communications in Australia. Intermediary’s business largely
focusses on the supply of unified communications software as a
service. AVI-SPL focusses on installation and integration services
primarily for enterprise conference room hardware, with some
unified communications software solutions ancillary to this.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between the 26North
Funds (via its interest in AVI-SPL) and Intermedia in the
supply of unified communications software solutions
b. Intermedia has a small presence in Australia
c. there are alternative suppliers of unified communications
software solutions in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Accenture Inc. (**Accenture**) proposes to acquire Ziff Davis, Inc.’s (**Ziff Davis**) connectivity business comprising various entities and assets (the **Target**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Questionnaire - Accenture - Ziff Davis' connectivity business
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Accenture - Ziff Davis' connectivity business

events.title: Accenture - Ziff Davis' connectivity business -  Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Accenture Inc. (Accenture) proposes to acquire Ziff Davis, Inc.’s (Ziff
Davis) connectivity business comprising various entities and assets
(the Target) through a securities purchase agreement (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Accenture, is a global professional services company
headquartered in Dublin, Ireland. It provides a range of professional
services designed to assist the public and private sectors to
navigate digital transformation, optimise operations, and build
advanced technological capabilities. In Australia, Accenture (through
its ‘umlaut’ brand) provides network assessment services, among
other services.
Ziff Davis (NASDAQ: ZD) is a digital media and internet company.
The Target comprises Ziff Davis’ globally active connectivity
businesses, including its “Ookla”, “Speedtest”, “Ekahau”,
“RootMetrics” and “Downdetector” brands. The focus of the Target
business is the collection and provision of network connectivity data,
which can be used by customers for marketing purposes or by
telecom operators’ engineering teams for network optimisation
purposes.
In Australia, the Target supplies:
▪ network performance measurement, monitoring,
benchmarking, and diagnostic software and tools;
▪ related data products, analytical insights and services,
including the collection and analysis of network
performance, experience and availability;
▪ reliability data across fixed broadband, mobile, satellite and
Wi-Fi networks; and
▪ enterprise wireless network management, planning and
optimisation solutions.

Accenture and the Target (together, the Parties) overlap in the
supply of network assessment services in Australia, comprising the
measurement and analysis of connectivity, performance and end-
user experience used to compare fixed and mobile broadband
networks/providers and diagnose relative strengths and
weaknesses.
In Australia, Accenture supplies network assessment services
through its umlaut brand, and the Target supplies network
assessment services through its Speedtest brand.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and other
information available to the ACCC.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• there is limited overlap between the Parties in the supply of
network assessment services in Australia;
• the market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low;
• post-acquisition, the merged entity will continue to face
competition from multiple alternative suppliers of network
assessment services.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Zurich Financial Services Australia Limited (Zurich) proposes to acquire all of the issued shares in ClearView Wealth Limited (ClearView) (the Acquisition).

events.title: Zurich - ClearView - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Zurich Financial Services Australia Limited’s (Zurich) proposed
acquisition of all of the issued shares in ClearView Wealth Limited
(ClearView) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Zurich, is an Australian financial services company that
is ultimately owned by Zurich Insurance Group Ltd, a company listed
on the SIX Swiss Exchange. Zurich, through its wholly owned
subsidiary, Zurich Australia Limited, supplies life insurance products
to customers in Australia through two brands, Zurich and OnePath
Life, including cover for life, income protection, total and permanent
disability, and trauma insurance. Zurich’s subsidiary, Zurich Assure
Australia Pty Limited, provides financial product advice to
customers, including independent advice in relation to life insurance.
The target, ClearView, is an ASX-listed company which, through its
wholly owned subsidiary, ClearView Life Assurance Limited, offers a
range of life insurance products to customers in Australia including
life, income protection, TPD and trauma insurance. ClearView
primarily supplies these life insurance products under its
ClearChoice brand and via the retail adviser channel (i.e. via financial
advisers).

Zurich and ClearView overlap in Australia in the supply of life
insurance products, and particularly in the supply of life insurance
products in the retail adviser channel.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third parties.
During consultation some third parties raised concerns, however,
these concerns generally related to broader structural dynamics and
commercial practices in the Australian life insurance industry rather
than concerns specific to the Acquisition.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The merged firm would continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of life insurance in the retail adviser
channel, including TAL, AIA, Acenda, Metlife, and NobleOak.
• While the merged firm would have a moderate share of supply of
life insurance in Australia, the increase arising from the
Acquisition would be low.
• The merged firm would be unlikely to have the ability and
incentive to foreclose rival financial advisers’ access to life
insurance products given there are alternative suppliers of life
insurance, as noted above.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: CleanPeak Energy Holdings Pty Ltd, through its wholly-owned subsidiary CleanPeak IFM Projects Pty Ltd as trustee for CleanPeak IFM Projects Trust (**CleanPeak IFM**) (together referred to as **CleanPeak**), proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Sustainable Energy Infrastructure Holdings Pty Ltd and 100% of the units in the Sustainable Energy Infrastructure Holdings Trust (**SEI**) from CareSuper Pty Ltd as trustee for CareSuper and State Street Australia Ltd as custodian for Australian Retirement Trust Pty Ltd as trustee for Australian Retirement Trust (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
CleanPeak Energy Holdings Pty Ltd and its wholly-owned subsidiary
CleanPeak IFM Projects Pty Ltd as trustee for CleanPeak IFM
Projects Trust (CleanPeak IFM) (together referred to as CleanPeak)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of CleanPeak IFM’s
proposed acquisition of 100% of the shares in Sustainable Energy
Infrastructure Holdings Pty Ltd and 100% of the units in the
Sustainable Energy Infrastructure Holdings Trust (SEI), as described
in the transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, CleanPeak, is a developer and operator of renewable
energy infrastructure, including solar farms and battery energy
storage systems, and a supplier of renewable energy and clean
energy solutions through solar and battery energy assets to large
industrial and commercial customers predominantly in New South
Wales. CleanPeak also provides asset management services for
compressed natural gas facilities on the premises of industrial
customers in Victoria, New South Wales and Queensland. In 2025,
KKR & Co. Inc. (together with its subsidiaries, KKR) acquired a
significant interest in CleanPeak.
KKR is a global investment firm that offers alternative asset
management as well as capital markets and insurance solutions.
KKR sponsors investment funds that invest in private equity, credit,
and real assets and has strategic partners that manage hedge funds.
The target, SEI, is a sustainable energy infrastructure investor which
owns a diversified portfolio of small scale (up to 5MW) solar farms
and battery energy storage systems (BESS) in Victoria, New South
Wales and South Australia as well as a compressed natural gas
facility located in Queensland.
The parties develop, own and operate renewable energy assets (in
particular, solar farms and BESS) and supply electricity generation in
the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Victoria, NSW and South
Australia. The parties also operate compressed natural gas (CNG)
facilities in Queensland.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between the parties in the
supply of electricity generation in the NEM in Victoria, NSW,
South Australia and nationally
b. the parties’ combined share in the supply of electricity
generation in the NEM in Victoria, NSW, South Australia and
nationally are estimated to be low and the increase in share
of supply arising from the Acquisition would be small
c. there are alternative suppliers of wholesale electricity
generation in the NEM in Victoria, NSW, South Australia and
nationally
d. there is no overlap between the parties’ operations in
relation to CNG
e. there appears to be a low risk of foreclosure or other
concerning vertical effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
TMX Markets Australia Pty Ltd (TMX), a wholly-owned subsidiary of
TMX Group Limited (TMX Group), applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the shares in
Middlebury Holdings Pty Limited (Middlebury Holdings) (including
its wholly-owned subsidiaries), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, TMX, is a special purpose vehicle incorporated for the
purpose of the Acquisition, and does not carry out any business
operations in Australia. TMX is a subsidiary of TMX Group, a publicly
traded company that operates globally and builds digital
communities and analytic solutions that facilitate the funding,
growth and success of businesses, traders and investors.
TMX Group’s key operations include Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX),
TSX Venture Exchange, TSX Alpha Exchange, the Canadian
Depository for Securities, Montréal Exchange, Canadian Derivatives
Clearing Corporation, TSX Trust, TMX Trayport, TMX Datalinx, TMX

The target, Middlebury Holdings, is the holding company for Cboe
Australia Pty Ltd (Cboe Australia) and Cboe Australian Services Pty

Cboe Australia is an ASIC-regulated securities market operator that
provides investors with access to local and global investment

listed equities, exchange-traded funds and structured products,
supported by the proprietary trading technology derived from Cboe
Global Markets’ international platforms.
Cboe Australia Services provides various technical services,
including software development and network management, and
corporate services to Cboe Australia.

Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered

had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition

considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:

b. there is limited overlap between TMX and Cboe Australia in
the supply of exchange services in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Pantheon LLC (Pantheon), a wholly owned special purpose vehicle of
Nuveen, LLC (Nuveen), applied for a notification waiver in respect of
its proposed acquisition of the entire issued and to be issued share
capital of Schroders plc (Schroders), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).
Following completion, Nuveen will hold 100% of the issued share
capital of Schroders and will have sole control of Schroders.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Pantheon, is a wholly owned special purpose vehicle of
Nuveen.
Nuveen is the asset manager for, and wholly owned by, the Teachers
Insurance and Annuity Association of America (TIAA). Nuveen is US-
headquartered and offers a comprehensive range of investment
solutions designed to secure the long-term financial goals of
institutional and individual investors. Nuveen has ~AUD 2.17 trillion
(~USD 1.4 trillion) in AUM as of 31 December 2025 – with
approximately 93.5% in the Americas, 4% in Europe, the Middle East,
and Africa (EMEA) and 2.5% in Asia-Pacific (APAC).
TIAA is a New York stock life insurance company that provides
secure retirements and outcome-focused investment solutions
primarily to US-based individuals and institutions. TIAA has ~AUD
2.33 trillion (~USD 1.5 trillion) in assets under management (AUM),
and administration and total statutory capital of ~AUD 74.4 billion
(~USD 48 billion) as of 31 December 2025.
The target, Schroders is a UK-based global investment manager
which provides active asset management, wealth management and
investment solutions. Schroders had ~AUD 1.68 trillion (~GBP 824
billion) of AUM as of 31 December 2025, with 12% in the Americas,
64% in EMEA and 25% in APAC. Schroders operates three core
business divisions: Public Markets, Wealth Management, and
Schroders Capital, which focuses on private markets, including
private equity and real estate.
Both Nuveen and Schroders supply asset management services to
customers in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the supply of asset
management services to customers in Australia is low
b. there are alternative suppliers of asset management
services to customers in Australia
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of asset management and wealth
management services post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds

deals_cleared.merger_name: FC Group Holdings Corporation & ors - Fluent Retail
deals_cleared.merger_description: Fluent Retail Pty Ltd (**Fluent Retail**) operates in the retail technology and enterprise software industry, supplying cloud-based distributed order management system software as a SaaS platform. Its solutions serve omnichannel retailers, e-commerce businesses, and grocery and fashion retailers, enabling real-time inventory visibility and order orchestration across multiple fulfillment locations.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
FC Group Holdings Corporation (FC Group Holdings), Fluent
Commerce MidCo Inc. (FC MidCo) and Fluent Commerce Holdings
Inc. (FC HoldCo) applied for a notification waiver in respect of a US
redomicile occurring in three stages:
• the transfer of all the Fluent Retail Pty Ltd (Fluent Retail) shares
to FC Group Holdings in exchange for equivalent classes of FC
Group Holdings shares, following which the Fluent Retail
shareholders will be the sole shareholders of FC Group Holdings,
holding the same number and proportion of equivalent shares as
they held in Fluent Retail immediately prior
• the transfer of all the Fluent Retail shares from FC Group
Holdings to its wholly-owned subsidiary, FC MidCo, by way of
contribution of capital without further issue of shares
• the transfer of all the Fluent Retail shares from FC MidCo to its
wholly-owned subsidiary, FC HoldCo, by way of contribution of
capital without further issue of shares,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Fluent Retail operates in the retail technology and enterprise
software industry, supplying cloud-based distributed order
management system (OMS) software as a SaaS platform. Its
solutions serve omnichannel retailers, e-commerce businesses, and
grocery and fashion retailers, enabling real-time inventory visibility
and order orchestration across multiple fulfillment locations.
FC Group Holdings, FC MidCo and FC HoldCo are vehicles for the
purpose of the Acquisition and do not engage in business or
otherwise supply any goods or services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal
consolidation, removal of a competitor, or change to Fluent Retail’s
competitive position as a result of the Acquisition.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
The ACCC also considered the potential application of section
51ABD of the Act. The application states that on the facts of this
matter, including the particular entities and structures involved, it
was not clear that the Acquisition fell within that provision. Having
regard to the particular circumstances, including the consequences
of non-notification in reliance on the exemption if it was found not to
apply, the ACCC considered it appropriate to make a determination
on the basis that the acquisition provisions in the Act apply to the
Acquisition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Sail Parent Holdco Ltd, an acquisition vehicle ultimately wholly owned by Constellation Software Inc. (**CSI**), a publicly listed company on the Toronto Stock Exchange, proposes to acquire a majority shareholding of around 90% of the issued equity interests in DerbySoft Holdings Limited (**DerbySoft**), with DerbySoft’s management retaining the remaining shareholding.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Sail Parent Holdco Ltd (Parent), an acquisition vehicle ultimately
wholly owned by Constellation Software Inc. (CSI), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a
majority shareholding of around 90% of the issued equity interests in
DerbySoft Holdings Limited (DerbySoft), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Parent, is part of the Vela Operating Group, a group of
operating companies owned by Vela Software International Inc.,
which in turn is a wholly owned subsidiary of CSI. The CSI group of
companies (CSI Group), including Vela Operating Group, acquire,
manage, and build industry-specific software businesses globally in
a variety of public and private sector industries.
CSI Group includes two operating groups which, among other things,
have teams focused on providing software to the global travel and
hospitality industries: the Juniper Group and the Jonas Group. The
services provided by these two groups include flight distribution
services, booking engine services, and digital marketing and
hospitality technology services.
The target, DerbySoft, is a B2B commerce platform operating in the
global travel industry. This industry involves platforms like DerbySoft
providing connectivity, digital marketing, and B2B marketplace
services to hotels, airlines, and other travel suppliers (e.g. tour
operators), connecting them with distributors and other channels,
such as online travel agencies, travel management companies,
metasearch platforms, and wholesalers across the globe.
The parties both supply digital software solutions and services for
the global travel and hospitality industries.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap or vertical relationship
between any of the operations of CSI Group’s portfolio
companies and DerbySoft in Australia
b. DerbySoft has a small Australian presence.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_declined.merger_name: Energy Bay - Rights to install/operate embedded networks and other assets from Centuria Capital Group members

acquirers.name: The Trustee for Energy Bay Kappa Fund

deals_declined.merger_description: Energy Bay Kappa Fund Pty Ltd as trustee for the Energy Bay Kappa Fund (**Energy Bay**) proposes to acquire from various subsidiaries of the Centuria Capital Group (the **CCG Members**):

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Energy Bay Kappa Fund Pty Ltd, as trustee for the Energy Bay Kappa
Fund (Energy Bay), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of:
a. energy infrastructure assets and the exclusive right to
operate existing embedded networks at 49 sites,
b. energy infrastructure assets and the exclusive right to install
and operate embedded networks at a further 211 sites, and
c. first and last rights of refusal to acquire analogous rights in
respect of any further sites of the kind described in the
transaction documents,
from various subsidiaries of the Centuria Capital Group (the CCG
Members), which is comprised of Centuria Capital Limited (CNI) and
Centuria Funds Management Limited (CFML), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.

The acquirer, Energy Bay, is a part of Energy Bay Group, which is a
distributed energy infrastructure company operating embedded
networks at large commercial and industrial sites in New South
Wales (NSW), Australian Capital Territory (ACT), Western Australia
(WA), Victoria (VIC) and Queensland (QLD). Energy Bay Group also
owns Diamond Energy Pty Ltd, an electricity retailer that operates in
all National Electricity Market (NEM) jurisdictions.
The vendors, the CCG Members, are subsidiaries of the Centuria
Capital Group, which is comprised of CNI and CFML. CNI is an ASX-
listed funds manager that creates, manages and invests in listed and
unlisted real estate funds, each of which holds properties for third
party investors. CFML is the responsible entity for the Centuria
Capital Fund.
In each of NSW, ACT, VIC, QLD, South Australia, and WA, the CCG
Members:
a. operate embedded network infrastructure,
b. engage in behind the meter generation and storage at their
embedded network sites, and
c. supply and sell electricity to commercial tenants connected
to their embedded networks.
(together, Embedded Network Services). Through the Acquisition,
Energy Bay Group will increase its share of supply of Embedded
Network Services in WA and the NEM.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has considered the
information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Act.
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition may raise competition issues. In particular:
a. there is meaningful horizontal overlap between Energy Bay
Group and the CCG Members in the supply of Embedded
Network Services in WA and the NEM, and Energy Bay
appears to be one of the larger suppliers of Embedded
Network Services in the NEM
b. the extent to which Energy Bay Group is likely to be
constrained by alternative suppliers of Embedded Network
Services post-acquisition is uncertain
c. there is a vertical relationship between the Embedded
Network Services supplied by the parties and the electricity
retailing operations of Energy Bay Group’s subsidiary,
Diamond Energy, and the impact of the Acquisition on
Energy Bay Group’s ability and/or incentive to foreclose
rivals warrants further consideration.
The ACCC considers that these potential competition issues warrant
consideration through the notification process, including via
consultation with third parties.
Given the above, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to reach a
concluded view on the likelihood of the Acquisition meeting the
notification thresholds. However, the ACCC notes that based on the
Australian revenue and transaction value figures provided, the
Acquisition may meet the monetary notification thresholds in ss 2-1
and 2-3 of the Competition and Consumer (Notification of
Acquisitions) Determination 2025.
For these reasons, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

targets.name: Donna Lea Buikstra Family Will Trust

deals_declined.merger_description: McCarroll’s of Moss Vale Pty Ltd (**MMV**) proposes to acquire substantially all of the assets of 2 separate and independently owned motor vehicle dealership businesses, Country Motor Company Pty Ltd (**Country Motor Company**) and Kinghorn Motors Pty Ltd (**Kinghorn Motors**), via a special purpose vehicle, MAG South Coast Pty Ltd (**MAG South Coast**). As part of the transaction, MMV, via McCarroll’s Property Holdings Pty Ltd, also proposes to acquire the land on which Country Motor Company and Kinghorn Motors respectively operate.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
McCarroll’s of Moss Vale Pty Ltd (MMV) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of:
• 2 separate and independently owned motor vehicle dealership
businesses, Country Motor Company Pty Ltd (Country Motor
Company) and Kinghorn Motors Pty Ltd (Kinghorn Motors)
(together, the Targets), via a special purpose vehicle, MAG South
Coast Pty Ltd (MAG South Coast), and
• the land on which the Country Motor Company and Kinghorn
Motors businesses are operated, via McCarroll Property
Holdings Pty Ltd,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.

The acquirer, MMV, is a new and used vehicle retail business
operating 15 franchised dealerships across locations in the Sydney,
North Shore, Hornsby, and Newcastle areas of New South Wales
(NSW). It is the authorised dealer of 15 original equipment
manufacturer (OEM) brands. It also supplies ancillary services
including parts, servicing, and finance.
The Targets, Country Motor Company and Kinghorn Motors, are
independently owned and operated, franchised dealerships located
in the Nowra region on the South Coast of NSW which supply new
and used cars, aftermarket parts, car servicing, and finance. Country
Motor Company operates three dealerships (representing Hyundai,
Isuzu, and GWM), and Kinghorn Motors operates two dealerships
(representing Ford and Volkswagen).

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, including the
parties’ revenues in Australia, the ACCC considers that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds would be
met.
The ACCC is unable to reach a view that the Acquisition, which
involves multiple independently operated dealerships, would not be
likely to raise competition issues in any market based on the
information provided. In particular:
a. the Acquisition would combine the separately owned
second- and third- largest independent dealerships and
reduce the number dealerships of new and used cars in
the Nowra region of NSW
b. the merged entity’s combined share of supply of new
and used cars in the Nowra region of NSW may be
significant
c. there is uncertainty regarding the extent to which the
merged entity would be likely to face intra-brand and
inter-brand competition from alternative dealerships in
the Nowra region, the surrounding areas, or in NSW
more broadly.
The ACCC considers that the Acquisition warrants consideration
through the notification process, including via consultation with third
parties.
Given the above, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
